subjects.subject: film-photography
subjects.title: Lenses for Film: Focal Lengths and Characteristics
subjects.lesson:
First principles
A lens is a field-of-view selector. On 35mm film, focal length sets how much of the scene hits the frame. Short focal lengths (24–35mm) take in a wide view; long focal lengths (85–200mm) take in a narrow view; around 50mm feels “normal.” Perspective itself is not created by the lens. It comes from camera position. To keep a subject the same size with a wider lens, you must move closer. That change of distance alters the relative sizes of near and far objects. What people call “compression” with telephotos is really the viewpoint you choose when you stand further back; background objects look larger relative to your subject because you’re farther away.

Field of view and distance
- Wide (24–35mm): Emphasizes space and context. To fill the frame with a person, you move closer, which stretches near-to-far distances and makes backgrounds seem smaller relative to the subject.
- Normal (45–58mm): Balanced, less distortion of space. Feels transparent.
- Short telephoto (85–135mm): Isolates. To frame a person similarly, you stand back, which closes near-to-far distance ratios and makes backgrounds appear larger and closer (“compression”).

Depth of field and rendering
Depth of field (DOF) is the range that looks acceptably sharp. For the same subject size in the frame: longer lenses need you to stand farther away, which increases DOF, but their larger entrance pupil at the same f-number can still create strong background blur. Practically: 35mm at f/2 shows more environment in focus than 85mm at f/2 when framing a head-and-shoulders portrait. Rendering also includes contrast, color, flare resistance, vignetting, distortion, and bokeh (how blur looks). Two 50mm lenses can feel different because of these qualities.

Primes vs zooms on film bodies
- Primes (fixed focal length): Usually simpler optics, often faster (wider maximum aperture like f/1.4–f/2), smaller, and with a distinct rendering. They teach you to see in that focal length and move your feet.
- Zooms (variable focal length): Flexible framing without changing lenses. On film SLRs, many classic zooms are slower (f/3.5–f/4.5) and may have more distortion or lower contrast than primes of the same era. Modern high-end zooms can be excellent but may be heavy and less common for vintage mounts.
Choose based on how you like to work. If you love low light and subject isolation, a fast prime kit (35/2 + 85/2) is great. If you value versatility, a 28–70mm zoom covers a trip well.

Using lens charts and MTF basics
MTF (Modulation Transfer Function) is a simple way to read how well a lens preserves contrast at different detail sizes (spatial frequencies) from center to edge. A value of 1.0 would mean perfect contrast reproduction; lower values mean more softness. You’ll often see curves for “low frequency” (coarse detail) and “high frequency” (fine detail), sometimes split into two orientations; beginners can read them as: higher and flatter curves usually mean better and more even sharpness.
How to use MTF practically:
- Compare center vs edge: Portrait shooters may care more about strong center contrast; landscape shooters want even edges.
- Look at high-frequency performance: Fine detail (like foliage or text) needs good high-frequency MTF.
- Consider aperture: Many charts are wide open; lenses often improve when stopped down 1–2 stops.
- Remember tool-chain limits: Film grain, enlargers, and scanners cap final resolution. Past a point, differences on paper charts may be invisible in your prints.

Field of view mental model
Think of your lens as deciding how much of the world you include, then use your feet to decide the perspective. Set focal length for story intent (context vs isolation), then move to place relationships in the frame.

Tool-chain limits
Your final image quality is a chain: lens -> film -> development -> enlarger or scanner -> paper or screen. A razor-sharp lens does not matter if your scanner is soft, or your film grain is coarse. Balance your lens choices with your film stock and output. For example, a vintage 35mm lens with pleasing flare might make a more emotionally powerful image than a clinically sharp modern lens when printed small.

Choosing a focal length family
- Street/travel: 28mm or 35mm for storytelling context; add 50mm for versatility.
- Portraits: 85mm or 105mm for flattering distance and background control.
- Landscapes: 24–35mm for breadth; add a 50mm for selective framing.
- All-round two-lens kit: 35mm + 85mm, or 28mm + 50mm.

Decision steps
1) Intent: What do you shoot most? People, places, details?
2) Light: Do you need a fast lens (f/1.8 or faster)?
3) Handling: Size, weight, focusing feel, minimum focus distance.
4) Evidence: Skim MTF to avoid weak corners if you need them; otherwise favor rendering you like from sample images.
5) Chain fit: Will your film and scanner show the difference?

Bottom line: Choose the field of view that serves your story, then learn the distance that makes perspective work. Charts confirm, but your intent decides.
subjects.example:
Stand your subject in front of a mural. Frame a head-and-shoulders portrait three ways:
- 35mm lens: You must stand close. The mural shrinks behind them; lines feel expansive. Background blur is modest at f/2.
- 50mm lens: You step back a little. The mural appears more proportionate. Blur increases at f/2.
- 85mm lens: You stand even farther back. The mural seems larger and closer behind the subject. At f/2, the subject pops and the mural melts.
Review the negatives side by side. You’ll see perspective changed only because of where you stood, not because of magic in the glass.
subjects.scenario: You’re packing a film SLR for a city-and-portrait weekend. You compare a 28–70mm f/3.5–4.5 zoom vs a 35mm f/2 and 85mm f/2 prime pair. You check MTF notes: the zoom is decent but softer at 70mm wide open; the 85mm prime is crisp in the center at f/2 and improves at f/2.8. You expect low light cafés and environmental portraits. You choose the 35/2 and 85/2. The 35mm lets you include context in narrow streets; the 85mm gives flattering distance and subject separation. You accept that you’ll swap lenses occasionally because the rendering and speed fit your intent.
subjects.reflection_question: If your goal is to make a small, emotionally warm 5×7 print of a friend at a café table, what trade-offs in focal length, distance, and lens character would you intentionally accept or even seek out?

subjects.subject: microeconomics
subjects.title: Supply—Determinants and Shifts
subjects.lesson:
First principles
A supply curve shows how much producers are willing and able to sell at each price, holding other factors constant. It slopes upward because producing more usually requires using costlier methods or inputs at the margin. A key skill is separating movements along the curve (caused by the product’s own price change) from shifts of the entire curve (caused by anything that changes marginal cost or producers’ willingness at every price).

Movement along vs shift
- Movement along: Only the product’s price changes. Higher price -> higher quantity supplied (same curve).
- Shift: A non-price determinant changes costs or capacity, moving the whole curve. Right shift = at any price, firms supply more (costs down or capacity up). Left shift = supply less (costs up or capacity down).

Determinants that shift supply
1) Input prices: If wages, raw materials, or energy get cheaper, supply shifts right; if they rise, supply shifts left.
2) Technology and productivity: Better processes, automation, or learning-by-doing lower marginal cost -> right shift.
3) Number of sellers and capacity: New entrants or added factories increase market supply; exits or capacity loss decrease it.
4) Expectations: If firms expect future prices to be higher, they may withhold current output (left shift today). If they expect lower future prices, they sell more now (right shift today).
5) Prices of related goods in production: For multiproduct firms or shared inputs, if an alternative product becomes more profitable, capacity may shift away (left shift). If complements in production (e.g., beef and leather), higher price of one can raise supply of the other.
6) Taxes, subsidies, and regulation: Per-unit taxes raise marginal cost (left shift). Subsidies or deregulation lower cost (right shift).
7) Shocks and environment: Weather for agriculture, strikes, supply-chain bottlenecks, geopolitics. Positive shocks shift right; negative shift left.
8) Time horizon: In the short run, capacity is sticky; in the long run, firms can adjust plants and processes, making supply more elastic and enabling larger shifts.

Production possibilities and opportunity cost
A firm’s feasible output depends on its resources and technology. If a resource is diverted to a different product or task with a better return, the opportunity cost of supplying the current good rises, shifting its supply left. Conversely, if a process innovation frees resources, opportunity cost falls, shifting supply right.

Comparative statics: clean thinking about shocks
Hold everything else constant. Change one determinant. Predict direction of the shift. Then infer new equilibrium price and quantity when combined with demand. Simple rules of thumb:
- Cost down -> supply right -> lower price, higher quantity (all else equal).
- Cost up -> supply left -> higher price, lower quantity.
- Capacity up -> supply right; capacity down -> supply left.

Common confusions
- A sale price falling due to demand weakness is not a supply shift; it’s a movement along the supply curve to a new price caused by a demand shift.
- “We produced more because price rose” is not a shift; that’s a movement along the curve.
- Inventories blur timing: A firm with stock can temporarily increase quantity supplied without a cost change, but sustained increases require capacity or cost shifts.

Reading real news with supply logic
- Chip yields improve: Technology up -> electronics supply right -> device prices ease.
- New carbon tax: Cost up -> energy-intensive goods supply left -> higher prices unless offset.
- Port closure: Logistics shock -> supply left for imported goods -> shortages and price spikes.

Elasticity note
Even with a rightward shift, if supply is inelastic in the short run (limited spare capacity), quantity rises little and prices fall modestly. Over time, as supply becomes more elastic, larger quantity changes absorb shocks with smaller price moves.

Putting it together
1) Identify the determinant that changed.
2) Decide direction of marginal cost or capacity change.
3) Shift the supply curve accordingly.
4) Predict effect on equilibrium given likely demand response.
5) Check time horizon and inventories to gauge magnitude.
subjects.example: A salad producer buys lettuce and packaging. A drought raises lettuce prices 30%. This raises the firm’s marginal cost at every output level. The supply curve for bagged salads shifts left. At the old price, the firm would lose money, so it reduces quantity supplied unless the market price rises. If later, drip irrigation tech spreads and lowers water usage, technology shifts supply right, partly reversing the earlier left shift.
subjects.scenario: You run a small bakery. Wheat futures signal sharply lower flour prices next quarter. Today, you keep output stable but plan to expand production when the cheaper flour arrives. Your expectation of lower future input costs doesn’t shift today’s supply (capacity isn’t here yet), but you schedule an extra oven installation next month. When the oven arrives (capacity up) and flour prices drop (input cost down), your supply shifts right. You can profitably offer more loaves at the same price, or cut price to gain market share.
subjects.reflection_question: Think of a product you buy often. Which single non-price factor, if it changed tomorrow, would most powerfully shift its supply, and why would that matter more than the product’s price moving along the existing curve?

subjects.subject: Microsoft 365 Administrator (MS-102)
subjects.title: Identity models cloud only hybrid and federated
subjects.lesson:
First principles
Identity answers “who,” authentication answers “how they prove it,” and directory answers “where the identity lives.” In Microsoft 365, the cloud directory is Microsoft Entra ID. Your choices revolve around source of authority (where accounts are mastered), how passwords or tokens are validated, and where trust boundaries sit. Simpler models reduce moving parts; complex models buy special control at the cost of fragility.

The three models
1) Cloud-only
- What it is: All identities are created and managed in Entra ID. No dependency on on-premises Active Directory (AD).
- Auth: Cloud-native (password hash stored in Entra ID, plus MFA, Conditional Access, FIDO2, etc.).
- Pros: Lowest complexity, fast to deploy, resilient to on-prem outages, modern security features by default, minimal infrastructure.
- Cons: Not suited if you need legacy on-prem Windows Integrated auth for file shares/apps, or if regulation requires on-prem credential validation.
- Fit: Cloud-first startups, small orgs without AD, greenfield mergers, departments carved out from larger orgs.

2) Hybrid (synchronized)
- What it is: Accounts are mastered in on-prem AD and synchronized to Entra ID using Microsoft Entra Connect Sync or Cloud Sync. Two main sign-in choices:
  a) Password Hash Sync (PHS) with Seamless SSO: A salted, hashed version of the on-prem password hash is synced to Entra ID; users sign in against the cloud. Seamless SSO uses computer accounts to enable silent SSO on domain-joined devices.
  b) Pass-through Authentication (PTA): The password is validated by lightweight on-prem agents in real time; no password hash is stored in Entra ID.
- Pros: Single identity across on-prem and cloud, retains AD-centric workflows, enables gradual migration, supports legacy needs while unlocking cloud features.
- Cons: Adds sync infrastructure and operational care. PTA depends on the availability of on-prem agents; PHS requires comfort with hash sync (secure by design, but a policy discussion). Some complex attribute flows need attention.
- Fit: Most enterprises with AD who are moving to cloud services while maintaining on-prem resources.

3) Federated
- What it is: A trust is established so that an external identity provider (often AD FS or a third-party IdP) authenticates users, and Entra ID accepts tokens from it.
- Pros: Centralizes advanced policies on-prem (smartcards, custom claims), keeps all password validation on-prem, supports niche protocols and complex SSO scenarios.
- Cons: Highest complexity and operational risk. If federation is down, users may be unable to sign in. Requires HA design (multiple AD FS and WAP servers, load balancers), patching, monitoring. Many needs once unique to federation are now addressed by Conditional Access and modern auth in the cloud.
- Fit: Strict compliance requiring on-prem validation, heavy smartcard mandates, or existing robust federation investments you cannot yet retire.

Tradeoff analysis
- Availability: Cloud-only and PHS are resilient to on-prem outages. PTA and federation rely on on-prem agents/servers; design for high availability or accept risk.
- Security posture: All models can use MFA and Conditional Access. PHS and cloud-only unlock the newest features fastest. Federation can enforce legacy constraints but broadens your attack surface if not well maintained.
- Complexity and cost: Cloud-only is simplest; hybrid adds moderate complexity; federation is complex and costly to operate.
- User experience: All can achieve SSO on domain-joined devices (Seamless SSO, PTA, AD FS). For roaming devices, cloud MFA and device compliance signals often matter more than the model.
- Compliance and data residency: Some regulations or contracts may require on-prem validation; that points toward PTA or federation. Otherwise, prefer simplicity.

Choosing a model
Ask:
1) Do you have on-prem AD and apps that must use it? If no, cloud-only. If yes, hybrid.
2) In hybrid, do you need on-prem password validation? If no, prefer PHS + Seamless SSO. If yes (policy or smartcard constraints), consider PTA; if you need advanced on-prem claims or nonstandard flows, federation.
3) What outage can you tolerate? If sign-in must survive WAN/datacenter loss, avoid federation unless you have robust HA and maybe backup sign-in paths.

Migration paths
- Federation -> PHS/PTA: Common to reduce complexity. Plan staged cutover, test Conditional Access parity, and retire AD FS after app claims are remapped.
- PTA -> PHS: Often to remove dependency on on-prem agents.
- Hybrid -> cloud-only: When all on-prem requirements are gone.

Practical notes
- Use Microsoft Entra Connect Health to monitor sync/PTA.
- Document source of authority and change control.
- Combine with device identity and compliance (Intune) to enrich Conditional Access.
- For smartcards: Evaluate cloud certificate auth or phishing-resistant methods before defaulting to federation.

Bottom line: Choose the simplest model that satisfies your control, compliance, and availability requirements. Complexity is a security and reliability cost—only pay it if you must.
subjects.example: A 300-person company has on-prem AD for file servers but is moving email to Microsoft 365. They choose hybrid with Password Hash Sync and Seamless SSO. Why? They keep one identity, gain robust cloud sign-in even if the office loses internet, enable Conditional Access and MFA, and avoid the operational burden of AD FS. File shares continue using on-prem AD while Exchange Online uses Entra ID. Six months later, as legacy apps retire, they can consider cloud-only for new subsidiaries.
subjects.scenario: A global manufacturer uses smartcards for plant access and Windows logons, with union agreements requiring on-prem credential validation. They also run several legacy apps that depend on custom claims. They adopt hybrid with federation (AD FS) to meet current constraints, designing HA across regions. In parallel, they pilot cloud certificate-based authentication and modernize claims in critical apps. As contracts renew and cloud-native auth satisfies requirements, they plan a controlled migration from federation to PHS to reduce risk and cost.
subjects.reflection_question: Under what concrete business or regulatory event would the extra complexity of federation stop being justified, and how would you detect that moment before an outage forces your hand?

subjects.subject: wisdom
subjects.title: Core Strengths and Limits
subjects.lesson:
First principles
You have limited time, attention, and energy. Wisdom is allocating them where they produce the greatest return. Comparative advantage says you create more total value by leaning into what you do relatively better and offloading what you do relatively worse—even if you could do everything yourself. Strengths are not just skills; they include what energizes you. Limits are not just weaknesses; they include constraints of biology, time, and context.

Categories to notice
- Skills: Things you do well now (analysis, writing, design, negotiation).
- Energizers: Tasks that leave you more alive after doing them.
- Drainers: Tasks that sap energy regardless of skill.
- Constraints: Fixed bounds (sleep need, chronic conditions, caregiving, work hours).
- Values: Lines you won’t cross; saying no preserves energy for what matters.

Inventory method
- Track for a week where your energy rises or falls. Circle the work that felt easy-and-effective vs hard-and-diminishing.
- Make a 2×2: Skill (high/low) by Energy (high/low).
  1) High skill, high energy: Your power zone. Double down.
  2) High skill, low energy: Efficient but draining. Systematize or batch; guard recovery time.
  3) Low skill, high energy: Raw enthusiasm. Train or pair up to convert into a strength.
  4) Low skill, low energy: Outsource, automate, or eliminate if at all possible.

Aligning tasks with strengths
- Design your default day around your power zone. Front-load it when energy is freshest.
- Batch drainers into tight windows with clear cutoffs.
- Pair complementary people: a detail-strong operator with a vision-strong strategist.
- Use environment design: Place tools, triggers, and friction to make desired actions automatic and undesired ones harder.

Mitigating limits (scaffolding)
- Checklists prevent avoidable errors when attention dips.
- Templates and scripts reduce decision fatigue for recurring conversations or emails.
- Guardrails: Alarms, calendar buffers, no-meeting blocks protect deep work.
- If-then rules: “If I’m asked for an off-the-cuff estimate, I ask for 10 minutes and run the numbers.”
- Timeboxing and stop rules prevent strong suits from crowding out everything else (overuse turns strengths into liabilities).

Growth vs fixed limits
- Trainable: A low-skill/high-energy area can become a strength with targeted practice and feedback.
- Manageable: A low-energy/high-skill area may stay a drainer; keep it small and structured.
- Non-negotiable: Sleep, health conditions, caregiving. Work with reality. Build schedules and teams that respect these.

Beware of overuse
A strength at 120% can become a weakness: analysis turns into paralysis, empathy into avoidance of hard calls, boldness into recklessness. Write a one-sentence “red line” for each strength: the point where its benefits reverse. Design early warning signals (missed deadlines, rising rework, avoidance patterns) and pre-commit countermeasures.

Decision lens
Ask of any task: 1) Am I the comparative-advantage person here? 2) If yes, how do I do it in my power zone? 3) If no, can it be delegated, traded, or scaled with a tool? 4) What scaffolding reduces risk if I must do it?

Review cadence
Once a month, scan your 2×2. Did your calendar match your strengths, or did drift and demands push you into drainers? Adjust assignments, renegotiate expectations, or change process. Small shifts compound.

Bottom line: Align work with where your effort is most valuable and sustainable. Mitigate, don’t deny, your limits. You’ll create more value with less burnout, and the people around you will, too.
subjects.example: You’re strong at data modeling (high skill, high energy) but drained by stakeholder workshops (high skill, low energy). You design a workshop script: a standard agenda, timeboxed Q&A, and a pre-read form that forces participants to rank priorities. You pair with a colleague who excels at facilitation; they run the room while you capture decisions and numbers. Result: Better outcomes, less exhaustion, and more time in the modeling zone.
subjects.scenario: A small nonprofit has three staff. One loves writing and donor stories but gets overwhelmed by bookkeeping. Another is meticulous with numbers but dreads public speaking. The director is a connector who energizes in meetings but struggles with long solo tasks. They remap roles: storyteller handles newsletters and grant narratives; the number-focused staff takes finance and CRM hygiene; the director leads donor meetings and partnerships. They add scaffolding: a monthly finance checklist, a shared outreach script, and a two-hour no-meeting block for deep writing. Fundraising improves, errors drop, and no one is consistently burned out.
subjects.reflection_question: Where are you currently overusing a strength to avoid a limit, and what single guardrail would keep that strength from turning into a liability?

subjects.subject: fiction-writing
subjects.title: Character Arcs 101
subjects.lesson:
First principles
A character arc is the inner change a protagonist undergoes under pressure. Plot is what happens; arc is what it means to the person. Most arcs fall into three types: positive (growth), flat (holding a truth that changes others), and negative (decline). Each arc can align with common plot structure so the external events force internal choices.

Core components
- Want: The external goal the character chases.
- Need: The internal truth they must accept to become whole.
- Lie: The false belief blocking the Need.
- Ghost: A past wound or context that planted the Lie.
The story squeezes the character until they face the Lie and choose Truth or double down on the Lie.

Arc types
1) Positive arc: From Lie to Truth. The character sheds a false belief and grows. Outcome: they act with new wisdom, often winning the external goal or reframing it.
2) Flat arc: The character already knows a core Truth. The world around them believes a Lie. The character’s steadiness changes others or exposes the system. Outcome: the world shifts; the character is tested but not transformed at the core.
3) Negative arc: From Truth to deeper Lie, or from naive belief to painful clarity. Variants include disillusionment (truth is bleak), corruption (character embraces the Lie), and fall (they’re destroyed by it).

Aligning beats with the Three-Act Structure
Act I — Setup
- Opening Image: Show life under the Lie.
- Inciting Incident: Disrupts comfort; stakes appear.
- First Plot Point (end of Act I): Irreversible entry into the story problem; the Lie-driven plan seems viable.

Act II — Confrontation
- First Half (reaction): The character tries to win using the Lie; partial successes and mounting costs.
- Midpoint: A revelation or mirror moment shows the cost of the Lie or a glimpse of the Truth; stakes sharpen.
- Second Half (proactive): The character experiments with the Truth but is not yet committed.
- Second Pinch: Antagonistic force exposes the weakness of half-measures.

Act III — Resolution
- All Is Lost: The Lie fails catastrophically; whiff of death.
- Dark Night of the Soul: The character recognizes the real Need and chooses.
- Climax: Action that proves the choice—adopt Truth (positive), hold Truth against pressure (flat), or embrace the Lie (negative).
- Resolution: New normal; show the consequence of the choice.

Blueprint you can draft quickly
1) Define Want, Need, Lie, Ghost in one sentence each.
2) Choose arc type (positive, flat, or negative).
3) Place four anchors: First Plot Point, Midpoint, All Is Lost, Climax. For each, state how the Lie is challenged.
4) Thread external pressures that force internal steps (antagonist, time limit, moral cost).
5) Track relationships: Who tempts toward the Lie? Who models the Truth?

Signals you’re on track
- Each big event forces an inner decision, not just a bigger explosion.
- Dialogue subtext shifts: early rationalizations sound confident, later they crack.
- The final action would have been impossible for the character on page one.

Pitfalls to avoid
- Sermonizing: Show choices and consequences; don’t explain the lesson.
- Unmotivated turns: Seed small moments that foreshadow the big choice.
- Arc/plot mismatch: If the external win requires selfishness but the arc pushes generosity, either change the win condition or accept a bittersweet ending.

Bottom line: Decide the Lie and the Need, then design events that make the character earn the Truth—or fail to.
subjects.example: Positive arc: Lina, a timid hospital clerk (Lie: “If I speak up, I’ll be punished; I have no power.”; Need: Courage to value lives over rules; Want: Keep her job and avoid conflict; Ghost: A past firing in her family for whistleblowing). Act I: A supply error endangers patients; she stays quiet. First Plot Point: A child is harmed; she takes a role in the audit, still silent publicly. Midpoint: She discovers proof of systemic fraud and sees a mentor take the fall—glimpse of Truth: silence enables harm. All Is Lost: Her friend is targeted; Lina is blamed. Dark Night: She realizes keeping her job means becoming complicit. Climax: She releases documents to regulators, speaks at a hearing, and coordinates allies. Resolution: The hospital reforms; Lina accepts a new role where speaking up is part of the job.
subjects.scenario: You’re outlining a mystery with a detective who already believes the Truth: justice matters even when institutions prefer convenience. That’s a flat arc. You design Act II so the department pressures the detective to close the case on a scapegoat. Midpoint: proof appears that the scapegoat is innocent. All Is Lost: the detective is suspended and threatened. Climax: they leak the evidence, risking career loss to force the real culprit into the open. The world shifts: a corrupt deputy is removed, policies change, and the detective remains fundamentally who they were—tested, not transformed.
subjects.reflection_question: What compelling Lie could tempt your protagonist at the climax, and how can you design earlier beats so that choosing the Truth will cost them something real?
